question:
Will GDDY's market close price on 2026-06-28 be higher than its market close price on 2026-06-21?

Stock splits and reverse splits will be accounted for in resolving this question. Forecasts on questions about companies that have been delisted (through mergers or bankruptcy) will resolve to their final close price.

Resolution criteria: Resolves to the market close price at https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/GDDY for the resolution date. If the resolution date coincides with a day the market is closed (weekend, holiday, etc.) the previous market close price is used.

Background: GoDaddy Inc. engages in the design and development of cloud-based products in the United States and internationally. It operates in two segments: Applications and Commerce (A&C), and Core Platform (Core). The A&C segment offers applications products, including Websites + Marketing, a mobile-optimized online tool that enables customers to build websites and e-commerce enabled online stores; and Managed WordPress, a streamlined and optimized website building that allows customers to build and manage a WordPress site; marketing tools and services, such as GoDaddy Studio and search engine optimization designed to help businesses acquire and engage customers and create content; and digital marketing services include email marketing, reputation management, and development of brand guides. This segment also offers Microsoft 365 that connects to customers' domains; email service plans with a multi-feature web interface; email backup, encryption, archiving, and other advanced e-mail security services; added security functionality services; GoDaddy Payments, a payment facilitator that enables customers to accept various forms of payments; Smart Terminal, a dual screen all-in-one point-of-sale system that allows customers to manage in-store inventory and product catalogs and accept payments; payment acceptance solutions; and online store capabilities. The Core segment offers domain products, including primary registrations, domain aftermarket platform, and domain name add-ons, as well as GoDaddy Registry, a provider of domain name registry services; and hosting and security services comprising shared website hosting and virtual private servers, as well as security products with a suite of tools designed to help secure customers' online presence; and SSL certificates. The company serves small businesses, individuals, organizations, developers, designers, and domain investors. GoDaddy Inc. was founded in 1997 and is headquartered in Tempe, Arizona.

Background: GoDaddy Inc. engages in the design and development of cloud-based products in the United States and internationally. It operates in two segments: Applications and Commerce (A&C), and Core Platform (Core). The A&C segment offers applications products, including Websites + Marketing, a mobile-optimized online tool that enables customers to build websites and e-commerce enabled online stores; and Managed WordPress, a streamlined and optimized website building that allows customers to build and manage a WordPress site; marketing tools and services, such as GoDaddy Studio and search engine optimization designed to help businesses acquire and engage customers and create content; and digital marketing services include email marketing, reputation management, and development of brand guides. The Core segment offers domain products, including primary registrations, domain aftermarket platform, and domain name add-ons, as well as GoDaddy Registry, a provider of domain name registry services; and hosting and security services comprising shared website hosting and virtual private servers, as well as security products with a suite of tools designed to help secure customers' online presence; and SSL certificates. The company serves small businesses, individuals, organizations, developers, designers, and domain investors. GoDaddy Inc. was founded in 1997 and is headquartered in Tempe, Arizona.

evidences.title: Did Strong Q4 Results and Upgraded 2026 Outlook Just Shift GoDaddy's (GDDY) Investment Narrative?
evidences.content: ## GoDaddy Investment Narrative Recap To own GoDaddy, you need to believe it can steadily grow revenue from domains, hosting and higher value applications while defending its small business customer base from bundled competitors. The latest quarter reinforces that story in the near term, with low double digit segment growth and better normalized EBITDA margins supporting the main bullish catalyst of improved profitability. However, the weak share price performance and underperformance versus the Nasdaq keep competitive pressure and customer churn as the biggest immediate risks. [...] The most relevant recent announcement here is GoDaddy's raised 2026 outlook for low double digit growth in its Applications and Commerce and Core Platform segments alongside higher normalized EBITDA margins. That uplift in guidance sits against a stock that has badly lagged the market over the past year, so it directly shapes the debate about whether earnings quality and margin improvement can offset concerns about slower long term growth and rising competition from integrated web presence and cloud providers. Yet despite healthier guidance, investors should be aware that intensifying AI driven comp...

evidences.title: does yahoo finance adjust for stock splits
evidences.content: What “adjusted” prices mean Adjusted prices are historical prices that have been retroactively scaled to remove the discontinuities that corporate actions create. When a stock splits, the raw Close price drops or rises by the split ratio on the split date; without adjustment, this creates an artificial jump in a time series and breaks return calculations. Back‑adjustment keeps a continuous, comparable price series by applying multipliers to all dates before a corporate action so prior prices are expressed in the same share basis as post‑event prices. The result is a cleaner series for percentage returns, charts, and statistical models. Split multipliers (how splits are applied) [...] + 2-for-1 split: multiplier = 1/2 = 0.5 — apply 0.5 to all prices before the split date. + 3-for-1 split: multiplier = 1/3 ≈ 0.3333. + 1-for-4 reverse split: multiplier = 4 — prior prices are multiplied by 4 (reverse splits increase prior prices when putting series on post‑split basis). Cumulative application: When multiple splits occur, multipliers multiply cumulatively. If a 2-for-1 split is later followed by a 3-for-1 split, the cumulative multiplier for dates before both events is 1/(2\3)=1/6 ≈...

evidences.title: Yahoo! Finance stock data gives different result from their graphs online - Stack Overflow
evidences.content: `7/23/2013 1696.63 1698.78 1691.13 1692.39 3096180000 1692.39` `7/1/1987 303.99 304 302.53 302.94 157000000 302.94` `7/23/2013 156.14 156.14 156.14 156.14 0 156.14` `7/1/1987 30.41 30.41 30.41 30.41 0 16.14` Topological Sort's user avatar ## 2 Answers 2 The reason for the discrepancy is because the graph plots the closing price (adjusted for splits), but you're comparing it to adjusted price, which accounts for splits and dividends. None of the columns in the historic dump show closing price, adjusted for splits only. Think of "adjusted price" as including dividend re-investment. Example: One way of looking at this is that between Jan 12 and 13, you lost 10%. This is what would be shown on the graph, with a little tick to show a dividend happened.

evidences.title: GoDaddy Inc. - Introducing GoDaddy Payments: Fast and Secure Payments for Small Businesses
evidences.content: ### Introducing GoDaddy Payments: Fast and Secure Payments for Small Businesses TEMPE, Ariz., June 15, 2021 /PRNewswire/ -- GoDaddy Inc. (NYSE: GDDY), the company that empowers everyday entrepreneurs, today announced the launch of GoDaddy Payments,, a new payments solution that enables GoDaddy Websites + Marketing and Managed WordPress WooCommerce customers to handle all of their commerce transactions directly through GoDaddy. GoDaddy Payments is built using the technology and teams acquired from Poynt in December 2020. [...] GoDaddy Payments provides a fast and secure way for GoDaddy's ecommerce customers to get paid. The setup process is simple and quick, enabling customers to begin using GoDaddy Payments for processing their customers' transactions in minutes. Payments are processed securely and efficiently, with funds deposited into users' bank accounts the very next business day. "GoDaddy is hyper focused on empowering our customers to sell everywhere with a single solution in a seamlessly intuitive experience," said GoDaddy President of Commerce Osama Bedier. "GoDaddy Payments represents a major step towards centralizing every tool and service a business needs to successfu...

evidences.title: yahoofinance - Is the historical volume data from Yahoo-Finance adjusted for splits? - Quantitative Finance Stack Exchange
evidences.content: I understand that the price data of the historical data from Yahoo-Finance is adjusted for splits. Is the volume adjusted, too? If it is not, the average volume should be accordingly higher after each split, because there are a lot more (or less) shares around. Or do I miss something? yahoofinance volume split Share Improve this question asked Mar 26, 2018 at 19:23 user31168 $\endgroup$ 2 $\begingroup$ Try finding an example of a split and seeing what happens to the volume as you pass through it. Might make it obvious? $\endgroup$ will – will 2018-03-26 21:17:10 +00:00 Commented Mar 26, 2018 at 21:17 $\begingroup$ Adding on to @will comment...also notice the price before and after the split on Yahoo. The answer is obvious. $\endgroup$ amdopt – amdopt [...] amdopt – amdopt 2018-03-27 12:38:36 +00:00 Commented Mar 27, 2018 at 12:38 Add a comment | ## 1 Answer 1 Reset to default 3 $\begingroup$ I believe the volumes are normally adjusted. Here is the yahoo data around Dec 23 2015 when Nike did a 2:1 split: Dec 30, 2015 64.36 64.40 63.17 63.25 61.49 5,817,900 Dec 29, 2015 64.31 64.48 64.02 64.26 62.48 6,708,600 Dec 28, 2015 63.21 63.88 62.80 63.81 62.04 8,704,400 Dec 24, 2015 64.55...
